courses.name: Academic Integrity Module
courses.description: The academic integrity module introduces academic integrity standards to inform and support students in avoiding plagiarism and academic misconduct. Students complete four parts that encompass academic misconduct and academic integrity decisions, including cheating, plagiarism, and collusion. Students learn about Turnitin (the text-matching tool) used at La Trobe University, and how to get help and support their development of referencing skills. To view instance specific details which include - Assessments, Class requirements and Subject instance coordinators - please select your preferred instance via the drop-down menu at the top right-hand side of this page.
learning_outcomes: Recognise and understand academic integrity responsibilities.
Locate, select, and utilise information about La Trobe's approach to academic integrity to support appropriate acknowledgement of references used.

courses.name: Wominjeka La Trobe: Indigenous Cultural Literacy for Higher Education
courses.description: Wominjeka La Trobe is a short one-hour compulsory online subject that introduces all La Trobe students to Indigenous Australian history, culture and customs. A rich and relevant cultural heritage education is important for everyone. This topic highlights the link between Indigenous knowledge and values and broader cultural literacy. It asks you to think critically about your own attitudes, values and beliefs. To view instance specific details which include - Assessments, Class requirements and Subject instance coordinators - please select your preferred instance via the drop-down menu at the top right-hand side of this page.

courses.name: Cryptography and Security
learning_outcomes: Explain basic mathematical principles and constructions underlying cryptographic methods and employ these to solve simple problems.
Identify and summarise a range of cryptographic techniques, including private key systems such as AES (Advanced Encryption System), and public key systems such as RSA (Rivest, Shamir, Adleman).
Identify and describe different contexts of application of cryptographic techniques in communication and cybersecurity, such as authentication and secure transmission.

courses.name: Cybersecurity in Practice
courses.description: Every organization and business that intends to protect itself from threats of cyber-attacks needs to know current practices in cyber defence. This subject will introduce common technologies used to identify and mitigate threats including firewalls, intrusion detection systems, intrusion protection systems and honeypots. This subject also introduces incident response processes and simple ways to incorporate secure programming practices to make computer programs less vulnerable to attacks. To view instance specific details which include - Assessments, Class requirements and Subject instance coordinators - please select your preferred instance via the drop-down menu at the top right-hand side of this page. Prescribed - Book - The Computer Incident Response Planning Handbook The Computer Incident Response Planning Handbook Resource requirement: Your full Reading List is available through Talis. The Library endeavours to provide free digital access to required resources so please check with your Subject Coordinator before making any purchases.

courses.name: Data-Based Critical Thinking
courses.description: This subject helps the student evaluate data-based evidence encountered in everyday life. It provides the fundamental numeracy skills required by business people, scientists, lawyers, nurses, journalists, social scientists, teachers and other professionals who need to evaluate data-based arguments, whether found in newspapers, television or on-line websites. This is achieved by a combination of studying newsworthy topics introduced in lectures, laboratory classes which encourage engagement with others and on-line quizzes that assess numeracy skills. The four themes covered in this subject are gathering useful data, turning data into information, probability and from data to decision making. To view instance specific details which include - Assessments, Class requirements and Subject instance coordinators - please select your preferred instance via the drop-down menu at the top right-hand side of this page. Prescribed - Book - Data-based critical thinking Data-based critical thinking Resource requirement: Your full Reading List is available through Talis. The Library endeavours to provide free digital access to required resources so please check with your Subject Coordinator before making any purchases.
learning_outcomes: Interpret/derive simple numeric and graphical statistical summary measures of data.
Calculate probabilities in a variety of scenarios that may be used for informed decision making.

courses.name: Inside Information Technology
courses.description: Students will be introduced to the rapidly expanding and influential field of information technology which comprises various types of technologies in computers (both hardware and software) and computer networks. It is therefore relevant to students in a wide range of disciplines: for examples, health sciences, business, engineering and technology. Students will be taught the fundamental principles of computer operations, different types of input/output and storage devices, computer networks including Local and Wide area networks, information systems, Database, the Internet, the World Wide Web and HTML. Students will acquire the knowledge and practical skills in information technology and be able to apply them to their field of study. To view instance specific details which include - Assessments, Class requirements and Subject instance coordinators - please select your preferred instance via the drop-down menu at the top right-hand side of this page. Prescribed - Book - Fluency with Information Technology: Skills, Concepts and Capabilities Fluency with Information Technology: Skills, Concepts and Capabilities Resource requirement: Your full Reading List is available through Talis. The Library endeavours to provide free digital access to required resources so please check with your Subject Coordinator before making any purchases.

courses.name: Introduction to Cybersecurity
courses.description: In the modern Internet era, industries and organisations need to be prepared to defend against cyber threats and attacks. Stakeholders should be familiar with basic principles and best practices of cybersecurity to protect their businesses and personal information. In this subject, these principles and strategies for future cyber security are explored. Key topics include: information security; ethical and legal practices; mitigating cyber vulnerabilities; and the process of incident response and analysis. The subject introduces the broad discipline of cybersecurity and outlines how to ensure the privacy, reliability, confidentiality and integrity of information systems and mitigate against cyber threats and risks. To view instance specific details which include - Assessments, Class requirements and Subject instance coordinators - please select your preferred instance via the drop-down menu at the top right-hand side of this page.
learning_outcomes: Analyse emerging cybersecurity practices, regulations, and standards, and evaluate their implications for digital security.
Apply and compare approaches to cyber risk management in addressing real-world digital security challenges.

courses.name: Network Engineering Fundamentals
courses.description: In this subject, students are introduced to the foundational concepts of computer networking. The subject covers the OSI reference model and its associated protocols to provide a layered understanding of network communication. Students will explore the lifecycle of a data packet across a network and learn the principles of IP addressing. The subject also includes hands-on experience with basic network device configuration and an introduction to static routing, enabling students to build and manage simple network topologies. To view instance specific details which include - Assessments, Class requirements and Subject instance coordinators - please select your preferred instance via the drop-down menu at the top right-hand side of this page.
learning_outcomes: Describe the overall OSI model and how layers interact with adjacent layers in the model.
Identify functions of each OSI layer and apply this knowledge to build a Network.
Describe the evolution of Local Area Network (LAN technology) with focus on Ethernet.
Apply appropriate network physical standards for different network scenarios.
Describe TCP/IP based network protocol suite and compare with OSI model.

courses.name: Object-Oriented Programming Fundamentals
courses.description: The Object-Oriented (OO) paradigm is a significant influence in software development in the computer industry. It divides a  system into objects that exist in the model of the application domain. In this subject, students will be introduced to OO concepts, terminologies, syntax and programming using Java. Topics covered include classes and objects, inheritance, object attributes and methods, overloaded methods, the use of the Unified Modelling Language (UML) for representing these concepts, primitive data types, flow of control, basic input/output, arrays, Java library class, and exception handling. Some software engineering principles are included: program design, algorithm for solving a problem and version control. This subject is designed as inclusive as possible for undergraduate/diploma students. To view instance specific details which include - Assessments, Class requirements and Subject instance coordinators - please select your preferred instance via the drop-down menu at the top right-hand side of this page. Prescribed - Web resource - Think Java: How to Think Like a Computer Scientist Think Java: How to Think Like a Computer Scientist Resource requirement: Allen B Downey and Chris Mayfield Your full Reading List is available through Talis. The Library endeavours to provide free digital access to required resources so please check with your Subject Coordinator before making any purchases.
learning_outcomes: Compare the traditional method of analysis/design/implementation and the object-oriented approach, with the OO model’s basic construct being the object, which combines both data structure and behaviour.
Apply the object-oriented method to designing and implementing a computer program for solving a real-life problem.

courses.name: Programming Environment
courses.description: In this subject you are introduced to the steps involved in designing and creating a software solution to simple real-life problems. To enable students to design a program, the subject introduces the way to break down the problems into smaller parts and analyse them methodically by using simple techniques such as drawing flowcharts. You will be introduced to the Python programming language as a means of creating executable program code to implement working software solutions. To view instance specific details which include - Assessments, Class requirements and Subject instance coordinators - please select your preferred instance via the drop-down menu at the top right-hand side of this page.
learning_outcomes: Analyse simple data processing problems to correctly identify the data and high-level processing involved.
Apply the 3 basic programming constructs of sequence, selection, and iteration to design computational solutions.
Use basic data structures such as lists and dictionaries to solve batch data processing problems.
Implement executable code in the Python programming language to solve computational problems.

courses.name: Cyber Law and Policy
courses.description: This subject introduces students to laws and policies regulating Cyberspace. It invites students to critically evaluate the sufficiency and effectiveness of laws, policies and industry self-regulation in addressing challenges to government, business and society in an increasingly online world. The subject specifically examines Australian and International Law and practice in this area paying attention to perspectives from legal, political and technological experts. To view instance specific details which include - Assessments, Class requirements and Subject instance coordinators - please select your preferred instance via the drop-down menu at the top right-hand side of this page.
learning_outcomes: Describe Security issues associated with the use of the cyberspace.
Analyse the application of laws and policies relating to cyberspace.
Evaluate the effectiveness and applicability of laws and policies relating to cyberspace security.

courses.name: Data Security and Information Assurance
learning_outcomes: Describe key concepts of data security and information assurance, including the CIA triad, information assets, and common organisational security challenges.
Identify and analyse threats, vulnerabilities, and risks affecting organisational information systems.
Evaluate security policies, ethical practices, and data maintenance methods to ensure compliance, confidentiality, integrity, and availability of organizational information.

courses.name: Human Factors in Cybersecurity
courses.description: This subject examines cybersecurity through a human factors lens across societal, industry, and organisational contexts. Students explore how ethical, social, and psychological factors shape human behaviour and cyber risk, and how these vulnerabilities arise and are exploited. They analyse cybercrime business models and global threats, with a focus on how human factors contribute to attack success and organisational risk. Building on this, students apply human factors principles to design organisational cybersecurity solutions, including cultural, communication, and policy strategies aligned to relevant frameworks. Through this progression, students develop professional skills in evaluative judgement, teamwork, and communication to deliver practical, human-centred cybersecurity solutions. To view instance specific details which include - Assessments, Class requirements and Subject instance coordinators - please select your preferred instance via the drop-down menu at the top right-hand side of this page.
learning_outcomes: Analyse psychological factors influencing cybersecurity behaviours and personality characteristics to develop strategies for secure practices and organisational sustainability goals.

Investigate social implications of human factors in cyber security, considering digital divides, socio-cultural norms, and digital empowerment, to inform policies promoting equitable access and participation in secure environments.

Analyse cybercrime business models and threats impacting corporate social and ethical responsibilities, assessing the influence of organisational culture, leadership, and communication on cyber security practices.
Assess the economic impact of and financial implications of investing in human factors in cyber security and propose cost-effective risk management strategies.

Develop a comprehensive cyber security culture, education and engagement strategy within an organisational context, whilst employing professional skills in evaluative judgement, team building, negotiation, and high-level communication across diverse environments.

courses.name: Wireless Network and Security
courses.description: Due to the ubiquitous nature of mobile and wireless technology in our daily lives and business operations, ensuring wireless security is becoming a top priority. This subject explores the security vulnerabilities in wireless networks and covers the fundamental concerns of confidentiality, integrity and availability. The following key areas will be covered: Wireless LAN Security - Wired Equivalent Privacy (WEP), Wi-Fi Protected Access (WPA), Authentication Servers (RADIUS), 802.11i security standard; Securing Ad-Hoc Networks - MAC layer misbehaviour, secure routing protocols, types of attacks and solutions, key management, broadcast authentication. To view instance specific details which include - Assessments, Class requirements and Subject instance coordinators - please select your preferred instance via the drop-down menu at the top right-hand side of this page. This subject contains career development learning or professional engagement opportunities

courses.name: Blockchain and Cryptocurrencies
courses.description: This subject introduces students to the principles and processes involved in blockchain technologies. The blockchain offers a way to secure transactions online between two parties, when there is no trusted intermediary available. A common use is in financial transactions without a bank as an intermediary, such as bitcoins and other cryptocurrencies. This subject covers the fundamentals of blockchain technology, including how the blockchain works, and how it is applied to modern digital transactions, including cryptocurrencies and smart contracts. To view instance specific details which include - Assessments, Class requirements and Subject instance coordinators - please select your preferred instance via the drop-down menu at the top right-hand side of this page.
learning_outcomes: Analyse different usecases and building blocks of blockchain and cryptocurrencies.
Demonstrate the use of blockchains and different cryptocurrencies to create a set of recommendations for appropriate usage by business and industry.
Explain different types of security measures in distributed ledgers to minimise risk.
Assess the commercial and legal environments needed for cryptocurrency start-ups to ensure business success.

courses.name: Intermediate Network Engineering
courses.description: In this subject, students will explore the structure and function of routers and examine key network routing concepts. The subject covers both static and dynamic routing, focusing on core protocols such as distance vector ,link state and, path vector ). Students will gain hands-on experience in configuring routers, building and interpreting routing tables, and evaluating the performance of these protocols across diverse network environments. To view instance specific details which include - Assessments, Class requirements and Subject instance coordinators - please select your preferred instance via the drop-down menu at the top right-hand side of this page.
learning_outcomes: Implement distance vector and link state routing protocols.
Implement multiprotocol network.
Recognize the strengths and weaknesses of routing protocols.
Broadly explain the scope of the TCP and UDP protocols.

courses.name: Introduction to Computer Forensics
courses.description: This subject considers the procedures related to digital forensic investigation in computing and networking scenarios. Students critically appraise the key concepts associated with data hiding, evidence collection and validation and traffic analysis required to perform forensic analysis in a digital environment. Students learn the importance of legal considerations in the documentation of forensic evidence. Case studies are incorporated for students to apply practical forensic tools. To view instance specific details which include - Assessments, Class requirements and Subject instance coordinators - please select your preferred instance via the drop-down menu at the top right-hand side of this page.

courses.name: Introduction to Penetration Testing
courses.description: Penetration testing encompasses the evaluation of organizational vulnerabilities by conducting thorough security testing. This subject provides students with a comprehensive understanding of the principles and procedures involved in system penetration testing and the ethical and legal obligations. It explores the utilization of popular software tools employed during penetration testing exercises. Through this course, students will acquire knowledge about different types of penetration testing and their respective phases, as well as gain expertise in interpreting results obtained from commonly utilized penetration testing tools. Additionally, students will recognize the significant value of penetration testing for businesses and organizations and will develop the skills to effectively report on and enhance an organization's security resilience using testing outcomes. To view instance specific details which include - Assessments, Class requirements and Subject instance coordinators - please select your preferred instance via the drop-down menu at the top right-hand side of this page. This subject contains career development learning or professional engagement opportunities
learning_outcomes: Apply penetration testing methodologies and techniques.
Identify and exploit common security vulnerabilities and recommend security controls.
Develop and produce client reports, explaining key findings to diverse audiences.

courses.name: Network Systems and Web Security
courses.description: In this subject you will explore current and emerging topics in Network Systems and Web Security. Beginning with an analysis of security attacks on system services and a discussion of both traditional and modern security mechanisms, the subject expands to cover security paradigms across various domains. The subject topics include system security, network security, web security, cloud security, and innovative approaches in cybersecurity. To view instance specific details which include - Assessments, Class requirements and Subject instance coordinators - please select your preferred instance via the drop-down menu at the top right-hand side of this page.
learning_outcomes: Analyse systems and networks for vulnerabilities and security flaws, incorporating modern tools and techniques for comprehensive analysis.
Apply cryptographic algorithms, including encryption methods, hash functions, digital signatures, and key exchange protocols, to secure systems, networks and clouds.
Examine and apply key security tools and protocols, such as Kerberos, PGP, IPSec, SSL/TLS, and emerging frameworks.
Identify and investigate ethical and legal considerations relevant to information privacy and security issues.

courses.name: Professional Practices and Entrepreneurship in Information Technology
courses.description: Entrepreneurship is an essential element of today’s society. This subject will outline the basic principles of Entrepreneurship. It will examine the steps required in developing an idea into a business and will explore the tools and necessary insights to make a successful venture. You will interpret and apply theory to case studies. Guest speakers will present and discuss start-up issues, pitfalls, and the ingredients for success. You will also develop professional skills related to ethical and moral decision making, and evaluate the social implications of entrepreneurship in the broader global context. To view instance specific details which include - Assessments, Class requirements and Subject instance coordinators - please select your preferred instance via the drop-down menu at the top right-hand side of this page. Prescribed - Book - The Startup Owner's Manual: The Step-By-Step Guide for Building a Great Company The Startup Owner's Manual: The Step-By-Step Guide for Building a Great Company Resource requirement: Steve Blank. Bob Dorf Prescribed - Book - Ethics in Information Technology Ethics in Information Technology Resource requirement: Prescribed - Book - Technology Entrepreneurship: Taking Innovation to the Marketplace Technology Entrepreneurship: Taking Innovation to the Marketplace Resource requirement: Thomas N. Duening Robert A. Hisrich, Michael A. Lechter Your full Reading List is available through Talis. The Library endeavours to provide free digital access to required resources so please check with your Subject Coordinator before making any purchases. This subject contains career development learning or professional engagement opportunities
learning_outcomes: Apply ethical theory to analyse ethical dilemmas in IT and business through debate.

courses.name: Project Management
courses.description: Project management is the process of leading the work of a team to achieve specified goals and outcomes in a specified time. This subject provides necessary skills and techniques to manage small or medium-sized information technology projects, with strong focus on the analytical side of project management, referring to scheduling, cost, and resource management, as well as the 'people' and client management issues that must be dealt with in order to ensure successful projects. In the subject you will work in a team to cover the skills required to assess, identify and manage risks to projects, to prepare realistic plans and appropriate documentation, track activities across project schedules and report appropriately to stakeholders. Your team's project will be assessed at different stages. You MUST complete both CSE3PM and CSE3CAP in two consecutive semesters. To view instance specific details which include - Assessments, Class requirements and Subject instance coordinators - please select your preferred instance via the drop-down menu at the top right-hand side of this page. Prescribed - Book - A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK® Guide) A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK® Guide) Resource requirement: Project Management Institute Project Management Institute Prescribed - Book - Information Technology Project Management Information Technology Project Management Resource requirement: Prescribed - Book - Communication for Business and the Professions: Strategies and Skills Communication for Business and the Professions: Strategies and Skills Resource requirement: Your full Reading List is available through Talis. The Library endeavours to provide free digital access to required resources so please check with your Subject Coordinator before making any purchases. This subject contains career development learning or professional engagement opportunities
learning_outcomes: Develop professional project design documentation to meet the requirements of software or cybersecurity industry standards.
Prepare effective and collaborative project team meetings through application of team dynamics knowledge.
Develop an IT project management framework that integrates security analysis and risk mitigation throughout the project's lifecycle.
Critically appraise, analyse, refine and document system requirements to solve real-time problems in software or cybersecurity systems.
